title: Dead of Winter: A Crossroads Game

walkthrough.title: Choose the Main Objective
walkthrough.text: Welcome to Dead of Winter. Before anything else, your group needs to pick a Main Objective card. These come in short, medium, and long game lengths. For your first game, grab a short objective — it will say so right on the card. Read the objective aloud so everyone knows the colony's shared goal. Place it face up where everyone can see it. This is what the group must accomplish to win, but keep in mind — it is not the only thing you need to worry about.

walkthrough.title: Set Up the Colony Board
walkthrough.text: Lay out the colony board in the center of the table. This is your home base — the place your survivors will defend and live. You will see spaces for the food supply, the waste pile, and the entrance where zombies gather. The colony board is the heart of the game, and most of your critical decisions will revolve around keeping it safe and fed.

walkthrough.title: Place the Six Outside Locations
walkthrough.text: Now arrange the six outside location cards around the colony. These are the places your survivors can travel to in order to search for supplies. You have the Police Station, the Library, the Grocery Store, the Gas Station, the School, and the Hospital. Each location has its own item deck and a limited number of survivor slots. Shuffle each location's item deck and place it face down next to its location card.

walkthrough.title: Prepare the Morale Track
walkthrough.text: Find the morale track on the colony board and place the morale marker at the starting value shown on your Main Objective card. Morale is the group's will to survive. It drops when bad things happen — survivors die, crises fail, people go hungry, waste piles up. If morale ever hits zero, everyone loses immediately. Well, everyone except a possible betrayer. Keep a close eye on this track all game.

walkthrough.title: Set Up the Round Tracker
walkthrough.text: Place the round marker on the first space of the round track. The Main Objective card tells you how many rounds you have. When the round marker reaches the end and the objective is not complete, the game is over and non-betrayers lose. Time pressure is real in Dead of Winter — every round counts.

walkthrough.title: Shuffle the Crisis Deck
walkthrough.text: Find the crisis cards and shuffle them into a face-down deck. Place it near the colony board. At the start of each round, you will reveal the top crisis card. Crises demand that players contribute specific types of item cards face down. If the group does not contribute enough, bad things happen. And yes, someone could secretly sabotage the crisis contribution. Trust is fragile in the apocalypse.

walkthrough.title: Shuffle the Crossroads Deck
walkthrough.text: Now shuffle the Crossroads cards into their own face-down deck. These are the narrative heart of the game. On each player's turn, the player to their right draws a Crossroads card and reads it silently. If the active player triggers the condition on the card — like moving to a specific location or performing a certain action — the reader stops play and reads the card aloud. The group then makes a dramatic choice. If the condition is never triggered, the card is simply discarded. Do not reveal untriggered Crossroads cards.

walkthrough.title: Deal Survivor Cards
walkthrough.text: Shuffle the survivor cards and deal four face up to each player. Each player picks two survivors to keep and discards the other two to the bottom of the deck. These are the people you control — each survivor has a unique ability, an influence value, and an attack value. One of your two survivors will be your group leader, which matters for voting later. Place your survivors' standees at the colony.

walkthrough.title: Deal Secret Objectives
walkthrough.text: This is the part that makes Dead of Winter special. Shuffle the secret objective cards. You need one Betrayer card in the deck per every two players, rounded down, plus enough Non-Betrayer cards for everyone. Deal one secret objective to each player. Read it secretly. Your secret objective is an additional personal win condition. Even if the colony succeeds at the Main Objective, you only win if your secret objective is also met. And if you got the Betrayer card — your goal might be to watch the colony burn.

walkthrough.title: Distribute Starting Items
walkthrough.text: Each player draws items to start the game. For each survivor you control, draw one card from the item deck of any outside location of your choice. This gives you a small starting hand of supplies. Keep your item cards hidden from other players — secrecy is part of the game's tension.

walkthrough.title: Stock the Colony Food Supply
walkthrough.text: Place food tokens in the colony's food supply equal to the number shown on the Main Objective card. At the end of every round, the colony must pay one food per survivor at the colony. If you cannot pay, each missing food costs one morale. Running out of food is one of the fastest ways to spiral toward defeat.

walkthrough.title: Place Starting Zombies
walkthrough.text: Add zombie tokens to the colony entrance. The Main Objective card tells you how many starting zombies to place. Then add one zombie to each outside location that has at least one survivor. Zombies will keep showing up every round, so barricades and combat are not optional — they are survival necessities.

walkthrough.title: Choose the First Player
walkthrough.text: The player whose survivors have the highest combined influence value goes first. If there is a tie, the tied player with the highest single-survivor influence goes first. Give that player the first player token. Play proceeds clockwise. The first player also has the tie-breaking vote during exile votes.

walkthrough.title: Ready to Survive
walkthrough.text: Setup is complete. Each player should have two survivors at the colony, a secret objective, a small hand of item cards, and a growing sense of unease about whether they can trust the person sitting next to them. The colony has food, zombies are at the gates, and the clock is ticking. Time to survive — or betray.

walkthrough.title: Round Structure Overview
walkthrough.text: Each round of Dead of Winter follows a specific sequence. First, reveal a new crisis card. Then the first player rolls action dice — one per survivor they control plus one extra. Each other player also rolls their dice. Then players take turns clockwise, spending dice and performing actions. After all players pass, the colony phase begins: pay food, check waste, resolve the crisis, add zombies, check the Main Objective, and advance the round marker. Let's break down each piece.

walkthrough.title: Reveal the Crisis
walkthrough.text: At the start of each round, flip the top crisis card face up. Read it aloud. Every crisis demands a certain number and type of item card contributions — things like food, fuel, or medicine symbols. Players will contribute cards face down to a shared pile throughout the round. At the end of the round, you will shuffle and reveal them. If matching contributions do not equal or exceed the player count, the crisis fails and its penalty triggers. Non-matching cards actually subtract from the total. This is where betrayal gets dangerous.

walkthrough.title: Roll Action Dice
walkthrough.text: Each player rolls action dice equal to the number of survivors they control plus one. So if you have two survivors, you roll three dice. These are standard six-sided dice. The numbers you roll determine which actions you can take this round. Higher numbers are generally better — a six lets you attack with guaranteed success, while a one can only clean waste. You will spend these dice throughout your turn to perform actions.

walkthrough.title: Actions That Require Dice
walkthrough.text: Five actions require you to spend an action die. Attack a zombie — you need to roll equal to or higher than the survivor's attack value. Search a location — spend any die to draw cards from that location's item deck. Barricade — spend any die to place a barricade token at your survivor's location. Clean waste — spend a die showing 0 or 1 to remove one waste token from the colony. Attract zombies — spend any die to move two zombies from one location's entrance to any other location. Each die can only be used once, so choose wisely.

walkthrough.title: Actions That Are Free
walkthrough.text: Several important actions do not require dice. You can play an item card from your hand for its effect. You can contribute an item card face down to the crisis pile. You can move a survivor from one location to another — but this triggers an exposure die roll. You can spend food tokens from the colony supply to boost actions. You can request items from other players. You can hand off items to another survivor at the same location. And the group can call a vote to exile a player. Free actions can be taken at any time during your turn, and you can take as many as you want.

walkthrough.title: Attacking Zombies
walkthrough.text: To attack a zombie, choose one of your survivors at a location with zombies and spend an action die that meets or exceeds that survivor's attack value. If successful, remove one zombie from that location's entrance. But here is the catch — after every attack, you must roll the exposure die. The exposure die can show a blank (safe), a wound, a frostbite, or the dreaded bite. A bite kills your survivor instantly and can spread to other survivors at that location. Combat is necessary but never safe.

walkthrough.title: Searching Locations
walkthrough.text: Searching is how you find the supplies your colony desperately needs. Spend any action die and draw cards from the item deck at your survivor's outside location. You cannot search at the colony — only at the six outside locations. Each location's deck contains different types of items. The Grocery Store has food. The Police Station has weapons. The Hospital has medicine. You draw the top card and decide whether to keep it or put it on the bottom of the deck and draw again. But each additional card you look at adds noise, which attracts more zombies.

walkthrough.title: The Exposure Die
walkthrough.text: The exposure die is rolled whenever a survivor attacks a zombie or moves to a new location. It has six faces. Three are blank — you are safe. One shows a wound — place a wound token on your survivor. They die after receiving three wounds. One shows frostbite — place a frostbite token. Frostbite is a wound that also deals one additional wound at the start of every subsequent round until the survivor dies or is healed. And one face shows a bite — your survivor dies immediately. When a survivor dies from a bite, the infection spreads: each other survivor at that location must also roll the exposure die. Bites cascade.

walkthrough.title: Moving Between Locations
walkthrough.text: Moving is a free action, but it comes with risk. When you move a survivor from one location to another, you must roll the exposure die. This means every trip out of the colony — or between outside locations — could result in a wound, frostbite, or a lethal bite. Think carefully before moving. Is the search worth the risk? Can someone already at that location handle it instead? Unnecessary moves are one of the most common ways new players lose survivors.

walkthrough.title: Contributing to the Crisis
walkthrough.text: During the round, any player can contribute item cards face down to the crisis pile as a free action. The crisis card specifies what type of items are needed — usually a symbol like food, fuel, medicine, or tools. When the crisis resolves at end of round, you shuffle all contributed cards and reveal them. Cards matching the required symbol count toward success. Non-matching cards actually subtract from the total. You need contributions equal to or exceeding the number of players. This is where trust breaks down — was that non-matching card an honest mistake, or sabotage?

walkthrough.title: Crossroads Cards
walkthrough.text: At the start of each player's turn, the player to their right draws a Crossroads card and reads it silently. The card describes a trigger condition — something like 'if the active player moves a survivor' or 'if the active player searches the Hospital.' If the condition is met during the turn, the reader interrupts and reads the card aloud. The card presents a narrative scenario with a choice, often voted on by the group. These moments are the soul of Dead of Winter — dramatic, unpredictable, and sometimes devastating. If the trigger never happens, the card is quietly discarded.

walkthrough.title: Colony Phase: Pay Food
walkthrough.text: After all players have taken their turns, the colony phase begins. First, feed the colony. Count every survivor currently at the colony and remove that many food tokens from the food supply. If you do not have enough food, each missing food costs one morale. Starvation spirals fast — if multiple survivors are unfed, morale can drop by two, three, or more in a single round. Keep the food supply stocked or face the consequences.

walkthrough.title: Colony Phase: Check Waste
walkthrough.text: Next, check the waste pile. Every item card that gets used or discarded during the round goes into the colony's waste pile. For every ten cards in the waste pile, the colony loses one morale. Waste represents the filth and despair of a cramped, decaying shelter. Someone needs to spend action dice cleaning waste throughout the round to keep this under control. The clean waste action requires a die showing zero or one — not always easy to come by.

walkthrough.title: Colony Phase: Resolve Crisis
walkthrough.text: Now resolve the crisis. Shuffle the contributed cards and reveal them one at a time. Count the cards matching the crisis symbol. Then subtract any non-matching cards. If the final total equals or exceeds the number of players, the crisis passes — sometimes with a reward. If not, the crisis fails and its penalty takes effect. Penalties range from losing morale to adding zombies to losing survivors. After resolution, discard all contributed cards to waste. Yes, crisis contributions add to the waste pile too.

walkthrough.title: Colony Phase: Add Zombies
walkthrough.text: Zombies keep coming. Add one zombie to the colony entrance for every two survivors at the colony, rounded up. Then add one zombie to each outside location that has at least one survivor. If a location's entrance is full of zombies and you need to add more, the excess zombies breach into the location — each breach kills one survivor and costs one morale. Barricades absorb hits before survivors do, so keep them up.

walkthrough.title: Colony Phase: Check Objective and Advance Round
walkthrough.text: Finally, check if the Main Objective has been completed. If so, the game ends and each non-betrayer player checks their secret objective — you only win if both the Main Objective and your personal secret objective are met. If the objective is not complete, advance the round marker. If the round marker passes the final space, time is up and non-betrayers lose. The betrayer wins if morale hit zero at any point or the game ended in failure.

walkthrough.title: Voting to Exile
walkthrough.text: At any point during the round, any player can call for a vote to exile another player. All players vote simultaneously — thumbs up or thumbs down. If the majority votes to exile, that player is banished from the colony. Their survivors leave the colony and can never return. The exiled player receives a new Exiled secret objective card and continues playing, but with different goals. Exile is a powerful tool against a suspected betrayer, but exiling an innocent player has consequences too — it costs morale and weakens the group.

walkthrough.title: Winning and Losing
walkthrough.text: Dead of Winter has layered victory conditions. Non-betrayer players win only if the Main Objective is completed AND their personal secret objective is met. If the Main Objective fails, all non-betrayers lose regardless of personal objectives. The betrayer wins if the colony fails — either morale hits zero or time runs out without completing the Main Objective. An exiled player wins by completing their Exiled objective. It is entirely possible for some players to win and others to lose, even among the loyal survivors. This is a game where personal agendas can doom the colony.

summary.title: Round Structure Overview
bullets: Reveal Crisis → Roll Dice → Player Turns → Colony Phase
Colony Phase: Pay Food → Check Waste → Resolve Crisis → Add Zombies → Check Objective → Advance Round

walkthrough.title: Welcome to the Colony
walkthrough.text: Welcome to your first game of Dead of Winter. In this practice tutorial, we will walk through a scripted round with five players. Each player controls two survivors at the colony, and everyone has a secret objective. One of these five players might be a betrayer — but nobody knows for sure. Follow along with the moves, even if you have fewer players. We will see smart plays, critical mistakes, and the constant tension of not knowing who to trust.

walkthrough.title: The Situation
walkthrough.text: The Main Objective requires the colony to collect a certain number of supplies before time runs out. Morale starts at seven. The colony has enough food for this round, but just barely. A crisis card has been revealed that demands fuel symbols — each player should contribute one matching card if possible. The clock is ticking.

walkthrough.title: Rolling Action Dice
walkthrough.text: Everyone rolls their action dice. With two survivors each, every player rolls three dice — two for their survivors and one bonus die. Player 1 rolls a 6, 4, and 2. Player 2 rolls a 5, 3, and 1. Player 3 rolls a 4, 4, and 3. Player 4 rolls a 6, 2, and 1. Player 5 rolls a 3, 3, and 2. These dice will determine what everyone can do this round. Higher is generally better, but even low rolls have their uses.

walkthrough.title: Player 1: Smart Search
walkthrough.text: Player 1 sends a survivor to the Grocery Store to search for food. They spend their 4 die and draw the top card from the Grocery Store deck — it is a food card. Player 1 keeps it. They also roll the exposure die for moving to the Grocery Store. The result is blank — safe. Player 1 contributes a fuel card face down to the crisis pile. A solid, productive turn.

walkthrough.title: Player 2: Fighting Zombies
walkthrough.text: Player 2 notices zombies piling up at the colony entrance. Their survivor has an attack value of 4, and they rolled a 5 — good enough. They spend the die to kill one zombie and then roll the exposure die. A wound. Player 2's survivor takes one damage. Not ideal, but better than letting the zombies breach. Player 2 also contributes a fuel card to the crisis.

walkthrough.title: The Crossroads Moment
walkthrough.text: Before Player 3's turn, Player 2 draws a Crossroads card and reads it silently. It says: if the active player searches a location, read this card. Player 3 decides to search the Police Station for weapons — and Player 2 interrupts. The Crossroads card presents a dilemma: a stranger is at the gates begging for shelter. Taking them in adds a new helpless survivor who needs food, but refusing costs morale. The group votes. They reluctantly take the stranger in. Another mouth to feed, but morale is preserved.

walkthrough.title: Player 3: Searching and Contributing
walkthrough.text: Player 3 spends a die to search the Police Station and finds a weapon. Good — weapons help with zombie defense. They contribute a card to the crisis pile face down. Player 3 claims it is fuel. Is it? Nobody can check until the crisis resolves. Trust is all you have in Dead of Winter.

walkthrough.title: Player 4's Mistake: Ignoring Food
walkthrough.text: Player 4 looks at the colony's food supply. There are only four food tokens left, and after adding the stranger, there are now eleven survivors at the colony. That is a deficit of seven food. But Player 4 does not do the math. Instead, they spend both useful dice sending survivors to the Police Station and Gas Station to search for weapons and fuel — useful items, but not food. Player 4 contributes nothing to the crisis either, claiming they have nothing that matches.

walkthrough.title: Why That Was a Critical Mistake
walkthrough.text: Food is the colony's lifeline. At the end of this round, the colony needs to feed eleven survivors and only has four food. That means seven morale lost to starvation — dropping from seven to zero. Game over. Player 4 had cards they could have added to the food supply or could have searched the Grocery Store instead. Always count the food supply against the number of survivors at the colony. If the math does not work, finding food is priority number one.

walkthrough.title: Player 5: Trying to Save the Colony
walkthrough.text: Player 5 sees the food crisis developing and scrambles. They move a survivor to the Grocery Store — rolling the exposure die, which comes up blank, thankfully. They search and find two food cards, adding them to the colony supply. Player 5 also plays a food item card from their hand directly into the colony supply. That brings food up to seven. Better, but still four short. Player 5 contributes their last fuel card to the crisis.

walkthrough.title: Player 1: Reinforcing Food Supply
walkthrough.text: Player 1 recognizes the food emergency too. On their remaining actions, they spend their 2 die to search the Grocery Store again and find another food card. They also play a food item card from their hand. The colony food supply now sits at nine. Eleven survivors minus nine food means two morale lost. Painful, but survivable. If Player 4 had helped, this could have been avoided entirely.

walkthrough.title: Player 4's Second Mistake: Unnecessary Exposure
walkthrough.text: Player 4 decides to move one of their survivors from the Gas Station back to the colony, even though there is no pressing reason to do so. They roll the exposure die — frostbite. Now that survivor will take one wound at the start of every subsequent round until healed or dead. Player 4 moved when they did not need to and paid a steep price. Every exposure roll is a gamble. Never move unless the benefit clearly outweighs the risk.

walkthrough.title: Colony Phase: Feeding Time
walkthrough.text: All players have passed. Time for the colony phase. First, feeding. Eleven survivors at the colony, nine food in the supply. The colony is two food short. Morale drops from seven to five. Starvation hurts. If Player 4 had contributed to the food supply instead of chasing weapons, this would not have happened. The group eyes Player 4 suspiciously — was it poor play, or sabotage?

walkthrough.title: Colony Phase: Waste and Crisis
walkthrough.text: Next, check waste. Several cards were used this round, but the waste pile is still under ten, so no morale loss yet. Now, the crisis. Shuffle the contributed cards and reveal them. Five cards were contributed for five players. Four show fuel symbols — matching. But one card shows a food symbol — non-matching. Four matching minus one non-matching equals three. Three is less than five players. The crisis fails. The group loses one more morale, dropping to four. Someone contributed a non-matching card. Was it an honest mistake, or did someone deliberately sabotage the crisis?

walkthrough.title: The Blame Game
walkthrough.text: Suspicion flares. Player 4 already failed to help with food and made a needless move. Now a non-matching card tanked the crisis. Player 3 claimed to contribute fuel but nobody can verify individual contributions. Player 4 said they had nothing matching — so who put in the food card? This is the heart of Dead of Winter. You cannot prove anything. All you have is behavior, patterns, and gut instinct. The group may want to call an exile vote next round.

walkthrough.title: Colony Phase: Zombies Arrive
walkthrough.text: Time to add zombies. The colony has eleven survivors, so add six zombies to the colony entrance — one per two survivors, rounded up. Player 1's survivor is at the Grocery Store, so add one zombie there too. The colony entrance is getting crowded. If zombies breach, survivors die and morale drops. Someone needs to fight or barricade next round, or the colony will be overrun.

walkthrough.title: Player 4's Third Mistake: Suspected Sabotage
walkthrough.text: Looking back at the round, Player 4 did not contribute to the crisis, did not help with food, made an unnecessary move that cost a frostbite, and generally acted in a way that hurt the colony. Whether Player 4 is the betrayer or just playing poorly, the group has good reason to consider exile. But exiling an innocent player also costs morale and removes a valuable contributor. This is the agonizing tension of Dead of Winter — you can never be sure.

walkthrough.title: Lessons from the Round
walkthrough.text: Three critical lessons emerged this round. First, always count the food supply versus the number of survivors at the colony — starvation kills morale fast. Second, never move a survivor unless the benefit clearly outweighs the exposure risk. Third, contributing to the crisis matters — a failed crisis hurts everyone, and non-matching cards make it worse. These are the fundamentals of survival.

walkthrough.title: Round 2 Preview
walkthrough.text: Heading into Round 2, morale is at four — dangerously low. Player 4's survivor has frostbite ticking. The zombie horde at the colony is growing. A new crisis will demand new contributions. The group needs to decide: call an exile vote on Player 4, or give them another chance? Meanwhile, every player still has a secret objective they are quietly working toward. The Main Objective is still incomplete. The colony needs to get its act together — fast.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: Congratulations — you have survived your first round of Dead of Winter. You have seen action dice in play, watched a Crossroads card create a dramatic moment, experienced a crisis resolution with possible sabotage, and watched Player 4 learn the hard way about food management, unnecessary exposure, and the importance of contributing to the crisis. Remember: count the food, avoid pointless moves, and always contribute to the crisis — unless you are the betrayer.

walkthrough.title: Before You Brave the Winter
walkthrough.text:
Before you dive into a full game, here are three things that can help.

First — check out General Tips for strategy basics and survival priorities.

Second — reference the Appendix for a quick guide to locations, items, and game terms.

And most importantly — ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Stay alive out there.

summary.title: Welcome to the Colony
bullets: Scripted tutorial with 5 players, each controlling 2 survivors
One player might be the betrayer — nobody knows for sure
Covers a complete first round with mistakes and lessons

summary.title: The Situation
bullets: Main Objective: collect supplies before time runs out
Morale starts at 7, food supply is tight
Crisis card demands fuel symbol contributions

summary.title: Rolling Action Dice
bullets: Each player rolls 3 dice (2 survivors + 1 bonus)
Higher rolls offer more action flexibility
Low rolls (0-1) needed for cleaning waste

summary.title: Player 1: Smart Search
bullets: Moves to Grocery Store, searches for food — success
Rolls exposure die — blank (safe)
Contributes a fuel card to the crisis pile

summary.title: Player 2: Fighting Zombies
bullets: Spends die to attack zombie at colony entrance — kills it
Exposure die result: wound (1 damage on survivor)
Contributes fuel card to crisis

summary.title: The Crossroads Moment
bullets: Crossroads card triggers when Player 3 searches
Stranger at the gates — take them in or refuse
Group votes to accept — adds survivor but needs more food

summary.title: Player 3: Searching and Contributing
bullets: Searches Police Station, finds weapon
Contributes card to crisis — claims it is fuel
No way to verify until crisis resolves

summary.title: Player 4's Mistake: Ignoring Food
bullets: Colony has 4 food but 11 survivors need feeding
Player 4 searches for weapons and fuel instead of food
Does not contribute to crisis either

summary.title: Why That Was a Critical Mistake
bullets: 7 food deficit = 7 morale lost = instant game over
Always count food supply vs. survivors at colony
Food shortage is the #1 colony killer

summary.title: Player 5: Trying to Save the Colony
bullets: Rushes to Grocery Store, finds 2 food cards
Plays food item from hand into colony supply
Brings food total to 7 — still short but survivable

summary.title: Player 1: Reinforcing Food Supply
bullets: Searches Grocery Store again, finds more food
Plays food card from hand into colony supply
Food at 9 — only 2 short instead of 7

summary.title: Player 4's Second Mistake: Unnecessary Exposure
bullets: Moves survivor back to colony with no clear purpose
Exposure die result: frostbite — recurring wound each round
Never move unless the benefit outweighs the risk

summary.title: Colony Phase: Feeding Time
bullets: 11 survivors, 9 food — 2 short
Morale drops from 7 to 5 due to starvation
Would have been fine if Player 4 had helped with food

summary.title: Colony Phase: Waste and Crisis
bullets: Waste pile under 10 — no morale loss yet
Crisis: 4 matching, 1 non-matching = 3 total (needed 5)
Crisis fails — morale drops to 4. Someone sabotaged?

summary.title: The Blame Game
bullets: Player 4 already suspicious for not helping with food
Non-matching card tanked the crisis — who contributed it?
Cannot prove anything — behavior and patterns are all you have

summary.title: Colony Phase: Zombies Arrive
bullets: 6 zombies added to colony (11 survivors ÷ 2, rounded up)
1 zombie at Grocery Store (occupied location)
Colony entrance getting dangerously full

summary.title: Player 4's Third Mistake: Suspected Sabotage
bullets: No crisis contribution, no food help, unnecessary frostbite risk
Betrayer or bad play? Group cannot be certain
Exile vote tempting but costly if Player 4 is innocent

summary.title: Lessons from the Round
bullets: Count food vs. survivors — starvation kills morale fast
Never move unless the benefit outweighs exposure risk
Contribute to the crisis — failed crises hurt everyone

summary.title: Round 2 Preview
bullets: Morale at 4 — dangerously low
Player 4's survivor has frostbite ticking
Exile vote decision looming, Main Objective still incomplete

summary.title: Congratulations
bullets: Completed first round with all major mechanics demonstrated
Saw Crossroads drama, crisis sabotage tension, and food emergency
Key rules: count food, avoid pointless moves, contribute to crises

summary.title: Before You Brave the Winter
bullets: Check out General Tips → survival priorities and strategy basics
Reference the Appendix → quick-reference for locations, items, and terms
Ask your personal GM Guide → tap Q&A for instant answers during play

walkthrough.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
walkthrough.text: Before we talk strategy, here are the mistakes that kill colonies most often. First, ignoring the food supply — players get caught up searching for cool items and forget that starvation drops morale fast. Second, making unnecessary moves — every exposure roll is a gamble, and frostbite or a bite can cascade into disaster. Third, not contributing to crises — even if you do not have the exact symbol, failing a crisis hurts everyone. Fourth, tunnel-visioning on your secret objective while the colony collapses around you. Your secret objective means nothing if the Main Objective fails. Keep these four traps in mind and you will outlast most colonies.

walkthrough.title: Food Is Priority One
walkthrough.text: Count the food supply at the start of every round. Count the survivors at the colony. Do the math. If you will be short, finding food is the most important thing anyone can do this round. The Grocery Store is the primary source of food cards, but some other locations have food items too. Do not wait until the colony phase to realize you are short — by then it is too late. Starvation can cost three, four, or even five morale in a single round if the colony has grown. That is a death spiral you cannot recover from.

walkthrough.title: Minimize Exposure Rolls
walkthrough.text: Every time you attack a zombie or move a survivor, you roll the exposure die. One in six chance of an instant-kill bite. One in six for frostbite that slowly kills over time. One in six for a wound. That means half the time, something bad happens. Only move when the destination has something you truly need. Only attack when the zombie threat is genuinely dangerous. If a zombie is at an outside location with no survivors, leave it alone. Every unnecessary exposure roll is a chance for the game to punish you.

walkthrough.title: Crisis Management
walkthrough.text: Every player should try to contribute at least one matching card to the crisis each round. Even if you are not sure you have the right symbol, contributing nothing is suspicious and hurts the group. Non-matching cards subtract from the total, so never throw in random cards hoping they help — they will make things worse. If you genuinely do not have a matching card, say so publicly. The group can plan around known shortfalls. If you stay silent and the crisis fails, you look like the saboteur.

walkthrough.title: Reading Other Players
walkthrough.text: Dead of Winter is a social deduction game layered on top of a survival game. Pay attention to what other players do, not just what they say. Are they contributing to crises? Are they helping with food? Are they making suspicious moves? Someone who hoards items, avoids contributing to crises, and takes actions that do not obviously help the colony might be the betrayer — or they might be desperately trying to complete their secret objective. The key is watching patterns over multiple rounds. One suspicious action could be bad luck. Three suspicious actions in a row is a trend.

walkthrough.title: When to Call an Exile Vote
walkthrough.text: Exile is a nuclear option. It removes a player's survivors from the colony and gives them a new exiled objective — which might be even more dangerous for the group. Only call an exile vote when you have strong evidence: repeated crisis failures, deliberate waste of resources, or actions that clearly sabotage the colony. Exiling an innocent player costs morale and removes a productive contributor. But keeping a betrayer in the colony is worse. There is no right answer — only calculated risks. If you must exile, do it early when the morale cost is more survivable.

walkthrough.title: Balancing Personal and Group Objectives
walkthrough.text: Here is the tension that makes Dead of Winter unique: even loyal players have personal agendas. Your secret objective might require you to hoard medicine or have survivors at specific locations. Pursuing your objective can look suspicious to others. The trick is to work on your secret objective in ways that also help the colony. Search a location that helps your objective and provides useful items for the group. Contribute to crises even when it costs you cards you need. If the Main Objective fails, your secret objective is worthless. Colony first, personal goals second.

walkthrough.title: Managing Zombies and Barricades
walkthrough.text: Zombies accumulate at the colony and at every occupied outside location. If the entrance fills up and more zombies arrive, they breach — each breach kills a survivor and costs morale. Barricades absorb breaches before survivors die, so keep a barricade or two up at the colony and at any location where you have survivors parked. The colony is most vulnerable because more survivors means more zombies each round. Assign someone to zombie duty — killing or barricading — and rotate that responsibility. Do not let the horde get ahead of you.

summary.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
bullets: Don't ignore the food supply — starvation spirals morale fast
Don't move unnecessarily — exposure die is deadly (50% bad outcome)
Don't skip crisis contributions — failing hurts everyone
Don't tunnel on your secret objective while the colony collapses

summary.title: Food Is Priority One
bullets: Count food vs. survivors at colony every round
If short, Grocery Store search is top priority
Starvation can cost 3-5 morale in a single round
Don't wait until colony phase to notice the deficit

summary.title: Minimize Exposure Rolls
bullets: 1/6 bite (instant death), 1/6 frostbite, 1/6 wound = 50% bad
Only move when the destination is truly necessary
Only attack when zombies pose a real threat
Leave zombies at unoccupied locations alone

summary.title: Crisis Management
bullets: Every player should contribute at least 1 matching card
Non-matching cards subtract — never throw in random cards
If you lack matching cards, say so publicly
Silence + failed crisis = suspicion falls on you

summary.title: Reading Other Players
bullets: Watch actions, not just words
Crisis contributions, food help, and move patterns reveal intent
One suspicious action = possible bad luck
Three suspicious actions in a row = a trend worth acting on

summary.title: When to Call an Exile Vote
bullets: Exile is a nuclear option — removes player and costs morale
Need strong evidence: repeated crisis failures, deliberate sabotage
Exiling innocent player = lose contributor + morale
If you must exile, do it early when morale can absorb the cost

summary.title: Balancing Personal and Group Objectives
bullets: Even loyal players have secret agendas that can look suspicious
Pursue your objective in ways that also help the colony
Colony first, personal goals second — Main Objective failure = personal loss
Contribute to crises even when it costs cards you need

summary.title: Managing Zombies and Barricades
bullets: Zombies accumulate each round — colony gets 1 per 2 survivors
Full entrance = breaches = survivor deaths + morale loss
Keep barricades up at colony and occupied locations
Assign someone to zombie duty and rotate responsibility

walkthrough.title: Action Dice Optimization
walkthrough.text: Your action dice are your most limited resource. With only three dice per round for most of the game, every die matters. Plan your turn before spending any dice. High dice are precious — save sixes for guaranteed attacks and fives for survivor abilities that require them. Low dice are perfect for cleaning waste, which only needs a zero or one. Mid-range dice work well for searching and barricading. Never waste a high die on an action that any die could handle. Think of dice allocation like a budget — spend the minimum necessary for each action.

walkthrough.title: Location Control Strategy
walkthrough.text: Each outside location has a limited number of survivor slots and a unique item deck. Controlling the right locations at the right time is crucial. The Grocery Store is essential early when food pressure is highest. The Hospital matters when survivors have wounds or frostbite. The Police Station provides weapons that make zombie combat safer. Rather than spreading survivors thin across all six locations, concentrate on two or three that serve your current needs. An empty location does not attract zombies, so pulling survivors out of locations you do not need saves barricade actions and exposure risk.

walkthrough.title: Survivor Ability Synergy
walkthrough.text: Every survivor has a unique ability, and pairing the right survivors at the right locations creates powerful combos. A survivor with a low attack value and a search bonus should be at a location where you need items, not at the colony fighting zombies. A survivor with combat bonuses should be posted at the colony entrance on permanent zombie duty. Some survivors have abilities that reduce exposure die risk or grant free actions — these are among the most valuable in the game. When drafting your initial survivors, look for complementary abilities rather than picking the two with the highest influence.

walkthrough.title: Crisis Prediction and Preparation
walkthrough.text: The crisis deck is not random — the same types of crises appear repeatedly. After a few rounds, you will notice patterns in what symbols are demanded. Smart players start stockpiling cards with common crisis symbols — fuel and food are the most frequent demands. Instead of using every item card immediately, hold one or two back for crisis contributions. If you consistently have matching cards ready, you guarantee the crisis passes from your end and you build trust with the group. A player who always contributes successfully is rarely suspected of being the betrayer.

walkthrough.title: The Betrayer's Playbook
walkthrough.text: If you draw the Betrayer objective, your goal is to destroy the colony from within without getting caught. The most effective betrayer plays the long game. Contribute to crises early to build trust, but slip in one non-matching card when the group would still have enough without you — this way, your sabotage is hidden among passing crises. Waste actions subtly: search locations that do not help the colony, take items you do not share, and let food supplies dwindle by not volunteering to help. If you get exiled, your new objective is often easier to complete. Some betrayers deliberately get exiled once they have done enough damage.

walkthrough.title: Detecting the Betrayer
walkthrough.text: Catching the betrayer requires tracking contributions and actions across multiple rounds. Keep a mental ledger. How many cards has each player contributed to crises? How many crises have failed, and who could have caused it? Watch for players who consistently search low-priority locations, avoid contributing to food, or make unnecessary moves that add survivors to the colony and increase the food burden. The most telling sign is when a player's actions consistently do not align with the colony's most urgent needs. Ask direct questions: what did you contribute to the crisis? What items did you find? Honest players usually answer readily.

walkthrough.title: Morale Management
walkthrough.text: Morale is your health bar. Experienced players manage morale proactively rather than reactively. Know every source of morale loss: survivor deaths, starvation, waste buildup, failed crises, zombie breaches, and certain Crossroads outcomes. Know the sources of morale gain too — some Crossroads cards and certain item effects can restore morale. Plan each round to minimize net morale loss. If morale is at three and you expect to lose one to starvation and one to zombies, that crisis absolutely must pass — contribute extra cards if you have them. A morale cushion of two or three gives you room for unexpected disasters.

walkthrough.title: Endgame Timing
walkthrough.text: The final round is where games are won or lost. By this point, you should know approximately where your secret objective stands. If you are close, make every action count toward completing it. If the Main Objective is nearly complete, the group needs to coordinate a final push — this is not the time for individual agendas. Watch for betrayers making a last-minute sabotage play: tanking the final crisis, hoarding food, or deliberately getting a survivor killed to drop morale to zero. If morale is low in the last round, protecting morale is more important than completing the Main Objective early. Dead is dead — you cannot win if morale hits zero before the objective resolves.

summary.title: Action Dice Optimization
bullets: Plan your full turn before spending any dice
Save high dice (5-6) for attacks and key abilities
Use low dice (0-1) for waste cleaning
Never waste a high die on an action any die could handle

summary.title: Location Control Strategy
bullets: Concentrate on 2-3 locations that serve current needs
Grocery Store early (food), Hospital when wounded, Police Station for weapons
Empty locations don't attract zombies — pull out when done
Fewer locations = fewer exposure rolls and barricade costs

summary.title: Survivor Ability Synergy
bullets: Match survivor abilities to locations and roles
Low-attack/search-bonus survivors → item locations
Combat-bonus survivors → colony zombie duty
Draft for complementary abilities, not just high influence

summary.title: Crisis Prediction and Preparation
bullets: Track which crisis symbols appear most (fuel and food are common)
Stockpile 1-2 matching cards for guaranteed contributions
Consistent contributing builds trust and clears suspicion
Don't use every item immediately — hold reserves for crises

summary.title: The Betrayer's Playbook
bullets: Build trust early — contribute to crises honestly at first
Slip non-matching cards when the crisis would pass anyway
Sabotage subtly: search wrong locations, hoard items, let food dwindle
Getting exiled can sometimes make your objective easier

summary.title: Detecting the Betrayer
bullets: Track crisis contributions and action patterns across rounds
Watch for low-priority searches, food avoidance, unnecessary moves
Ask direct questions about contributions — honest players answer readily
One bad round = bad luck; consistent misalignment = betrayer

summary.title: Morale Management
bullets: Know every morale source: deaths, starvation, waste, failed crises, breaches
Plan each round to minimize net morale loss
Maintain a cushion of 2-3 morale for unexpected disasters
Some Crossroads cards and items can restore morale

summary.title: Endgame Timing
bullets: Final round: coordinate group push on Main Objective
Complete your secret objective before or during the last round
Watch for last-minute betrayer sabotage on crisis or morale
Protecting morale > completing objective early if morale is critical

entries.definition: A character card with unique abilities, an attack value, an influence value, and a search ability. Each player controls two or more survivors throughout the game. Survivors can be at the colony or at outside locations.
Each player's starting survivor with the highest influence is their leader. Leaders matter for certain game effects and represent the player's primary character in the colony.
A number on each survivor card representing their social standing and leadership ability. Used to determine first player (highest combined influence) and for certain game effects.

A number on each survivor card showing the minimum die roll needed to successfully kill a zombie. Lower attack values are better — a survivor with attack value 2 can kill zombies more easily than one with attack value 5.

Each survivor has a unique special ability printed on their card. Abilities range from combat bonuses to free search actions to exposure die protection. Some abilities are passive, others require activation.

Standard six-sided dice rolled at the start of each round. Each player rolls dice equal to their number of survivors plus one. Dice are spent throughout the turn to perform actions like attacking, searching, barricading, cleaning waste, and attracting zombies.
Die results of 5 or 6 are the most flexible. They meet almost any survivor's attack value and can be used for any dice action. Save these for attacks or critical actions.
Die results of 0 or 1 are required for the Clean Waste action. They are less useful for attacks but serve an important housekeeping function. The 0 face exists on the custom dice.

A twelve-sided die rolled whenever a survivor attacks a zombie or moves between locations. Has 6 blank faces (safe), 2 wound faces, 2 frostbite faces, and 2 bite faces. The bite face kills instantly and can chain to other survivors.

A damage token placed on a survivor. A survivor dies after receiving three wound tokens. Wounds can come from the exposure die, zombie breaches, and certain Crossroads events. Some items and abilities can heal wounds.

A special wound from the exposure die that deals one additional wound at the start of every subsequent round. A survivor with frostbite will slowly die unless healed. Frostbite tokens count as wounds for the three-wound death threshold.

The deadliest exposure die result. A bitten survivor dies immediately — no saves, no healing. When a survivor dies from a bite, every other survivor at that same location must also roll the exposure die. Bites can cascade through multiple survivors at a location.

The central board representing the survivors' home base. Contains the food supply, waste pile, and zombie entrance. Most survivors start here, and feeding only applies to survivors at the colony. The colony entrance has limited zombie slots before breaching occurs.
An outside location whose item deck contains weapons, ammunition, and some defensive items. Good for arming survivors for zombie combat. Limited survivor slots.
An outside location whose item deck is heavily weighted toward food cards. The most important early-game location for keeping the colony fed. Food cards can also be contributed to food-symbol crises.
An outside location whose item deck contains medicine and healing items. Critical when survivors have wounds or frostbite. Medicine cards can cure frostbite and heal wounds.
An outside location whose item deck contains blueprint and knowledge cards. Useful for special abilities and colony improvements. Often a lower priority than food and weapon locations.
An outside location whose item deck contains fuel cards. Fuel is a common crisis symbol and is needed for certain Main Objectives. Also contains some utility items.
An outside location whose item deck contains a mix of item types. Useful for finding specific cards when other locations are depleted or occupied. Contains some food and tool items.

Cards drawn from outside location decks through searching. Items can be played for their effects, contributed to crises, or added to the colony food supply. Each item shows one or more symbols (food, fuel, medicine, etc.) and may have a special ability when played.

Item cards showing the food symbol. Can be added to the colony food supply to feed survivors during the colony phase. Primarily found at the Grocery Store. One of the most important item types in the game.

Item cards that improve combat effectiveness. Some weapons lower a survivor's attack value, making it easier to kill zombies. Others deal damage without requiring an exposure die roll. Found primarily at the Police Station.

Item cards that can heal wounds or cure frostbite on survivors. Found primarily at the Hospital. Critical for keeping wounded survivors alive, especially those suffering from frostbite's recurring damage.
Item cards showing the fuel symbol. Commonly demanded by crisis cards. Also required for certain Main Objectives and special actions. Found primarily at the Gas Station.

A card revealed at the start of each round that demands the group contribute item cards with specific symbols. Players contribute face down, cards are shuffled and revealed at end of round. Matching cards count toward passing; non-matching cards subtract. Need total >= player count to pass.

A free action where a player places an item card face down into the crisis pile. Contributions are anonymous until revealed. Each player may contribute one card per turn. Matching symbols help; non-matching symbols hurt. This anonymity is what enables betrayer sabotage.

When the total matching contributions minus non-matching cards is less than the player count, the crisis fails. Each crisis card specifies its own failure penalty — common penalties include morale loss, zombie additions, survivor wounds, or food loss.

A narrative event card drawn by the player to the active player's right. Contains a hidden trigger condition. If triggered during the active player's turn, the card is read aloud and presents a dramatic choice — often voted on by the group. Crossroads events can be beneficial, harmful, or bittersweet.

The specific condition on a Crossroads card that must be met for the event to activate. Triggers can include moving to a specific location, searching, attacking, or having a specific survivor in play. If the trigger never occurs during the turn, the card is discarded without being revealed.

A track on the colony board representing the group's collective will to survive. Starts at a value set by the Main Objective card. Decreases from survivor deaths, starvation, waste, failed crises, and zombie breaches. If morale reaches zero, the game ends immediately and non-betrayers lose.

Morale drops from: survivor death (-1 each), starvation (-1 per unfed survivor at colony), waste pile (every 10 cards = -1), failed crises (penalty varies), zombie breaches (-1 each), and certain Crossroads events. Managing all these sources simultaneously is the key to survival.

A shared win condition visible to all players. Specifies what the colony must accomplish (collect items, kill zombies, survive rounds, etc.) and sets the game length, starting morale, and starting food. Non-betrayer players must complete the Main Objective to have any chance of winning.

A hidden personal win condition dealt to each player during setup. Even non-betrayer secret objectives require specific accomplishments beyond the Main Objective — such as having certain items, survivors at specific locations, or resources stockpiled. You must complete both the Main Objective and your secret objective to win.

A player whose secret objective is to sabotage the colony. The betrayer wins if morale drops to zero or the Main Objective is not completed by game end. Betrayers benefit from subtle sabotage — contributing non-matching crisis cards, hoarding resources, and making wasteful actions that look accidental.

A player removed from the colony by majority vote. Exiled players receive a new Exiled secret objective and continue playing with different win conditions. Their survivors can no longer enter the colony. Exiling costs morale and should only be done with strong evidence of betrayal.

A new secret objective given to an exiled player, replacing their original one. Exiled objectives typically require the player to accomplish specific goals while operating outside the colony. Some exiled objectives are easier to complete than the original betrayer objective.

A pool of food tokens at the colony. During the colony phase, one food is consumed per survivor at the colony. If food runs out, each unfed survivor costs one morale. Food is replenished by playing food item cards into the supply. The most critical resource to manage.

A pile of discarded item cards at the colony. Every used or discarded item card goes here, including cards played for their effects and crisis contributions after resolution. For every ten cards in the waste pile, the colony loses one morale during the colony phase. The Clean Waste action removes cards from this pile.

Enemy tokens that appear at the colony and outside locations each round. The colony receives one zombie per two survivors (rounded up). Each occupied outside location gets one zombie. If a location's entrance is full when adding zombies, excess zombies breach — each breach kills one survivor and costs one morale.

When zombies are added to a location whose entrance is already full, each excess zombie breaches the location. Each breach removes one barricade first. If no barricades remain, each breach kills one survivor at that location and costs one morale. Breaches are one of the deadliest threats in the game.

A defensive token placed at a location's entrance by spending an action die. Barricades absorb zombie breaches before survivors are killed. Each barricade absorbs one breach and is then removed. Maintaining barricades at the colony and occupied locations is essential for survival.

Generated when a survivor searches a location and looks at additional cards beyond the first. Each extra card creates noise, which attracts additional zombies to that location. Noise makes extended searching risky — finding the perfect card might bring a horde to your doorstep.
